As I’ll mention below, Brazil and coffee tariffs play very important roles in the company’s, and how they will continue to navigate what they call a “very challenging environment” will be very important for the stock price.

Dividends & Buybacks

Currently, J.M. Smucker is a Dividend Aristocrat, with a nearly 4% forward yield - one of the highest they’ve traded at in recent years on the back of the falling stock price. The company aims at maintaining a 40-45% adjusted EPS payout target, although I prefer looking at the free cash flow. Based on last fiscal year’s free cash flow, the $455.4 million dividend represents a ~55.75% payout, which is not bad at all, allowing the company to increase it further before it becomes an issue. AJM is aiming for a balance between debt repayments, investments, dividends and buybacks, which I think is optimal especially given the current market conditions.

Risks & Opportunities

It’s important to mention that SJM is not the only company in the industry that’s falling, despite overall solid numbers even in this uncertain environment. As mentioned, they have many discretionary products in their portfolio, which people can easily cut on in bad times. A little side note: when working in the pet food retail industry during the pandemic, I’ve seen a significant shift from our premium and ultra-premium products towards the cheaper alternatives, as people either maintained the same budget (while everyone was growing prices) or even cut their spending on pet food. This was actually just the tip of the iceberg, with the trend being visible in pretty much every other industry especially following the post-pandemic wave of inflation, which many companies are still recovering from - think about Nike (NKE), Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMHF) or even PepsiCo (PEP) and most of their competitors, all of which have been reporting issues in consumer demand in recent years.

Another issue that’s tied to their discretionary products are high rates and their effects. By increasing the interest rates, the Fed basically lowers discretionary budgets, forcing people to tighten the belt, which then results in lower inflation, as we’ve seen over the recent years. At the same time, rate cuts can have the opposite effect, increasing these discretionary budgets by lowering the cost of debt of their consumers (i.e. mortgage, credit card costs, etc.). Plus, they can be great for dividend stocks, as the market would have to risk up if it wants to get 4-5% yields that the current nearly risk-free treasuries pay, as well as lowering SJM’s interest payments and making refinancing and investing using debt a much more appealing option.

As for another opportunity, I have to mention the potential for an international expansion. Right now, most of their sales come from the US, with a very small (3.58% of last year’s net sales) coming from Canada, especially after they even divested a condiment business there last year, and ~1.37% of sales coming from other international markets - so, not even 5% of the total sales. This category is so small that they include it in another subcategory, which includes their away from home segment (selling products in restaurants, cafeterias, schools, offices, hotels, etc.), and it could represent an opportunity in the long-term, while the US market could remain the slow cash flow producer.

However, regarding that, tariffs remain a very important near-term uncertainty that recently made them crash, as SJM imports huge amounts of commodities, especially green coffee beans, with coffee being their largest revenue contributor. As Trump said that the US will impose a 50% tariff on imports from Brazil starting in August, SJM is expecting this to be one of their biggest impact drivers, saying that tariffs plus the inflation in coffee prices (that they also help generate) could account for a hit of about $1 to their Adjusted EPS midpoint for FY26, meaning about a 10% hit. I don’t think tariffs are a long-term goal but rather a tool for near-term trade deals, as it’s visible especially following the US-EU deal announced recently. It’s still uncertain, but more clarity in that can result in significant drops in commodity prices (see copper dropping 20% in a day following more clarity on copper tariffs), so the impact may not be that high overall, depending on their timing. Still, SJM is expecting about ~20% net pricing effects (i.e. increasing the prices) as a result, partially offset by ~10% lower volumes expected as a result of coffee’s relatively inelastic demand.

Also, competition ultimately remains a key long-term issue. SJM has strong brands, but so do many of their competitors, and with so many niche products on top, it’s very hard to compete in this market. This is why they have to spend nearly half a billion ($468.2 million last fiscal year) on marketing, which is more than their CAPEX for the year.

Also, the “Make America Healthy Again” policies could hurt SJM as well in the future, as many of their products could even be considered junk food, which this administration seems to be targeting. Plus, the significant growth in GLP-1 (think about Ozempic and Wegovy) use for weight loss could affect SJM especially prior to the 2023 acquisition of Hostess Brands. By reducing cravings especially for the more “discretionary” items like snacks, the timing of this acquisition may end up being very unfortunate. It’s not just that, but after going through this weight loss process, there’s the risk (a risk for SJM and their competitors) that people may be much more mindful about what they put in their body, which can be good for some of the brands (like Jif) and bad especially for the snacks.

However, they are adapting to these changes, recently announcing that they will remove food colorings by the end of 2027 in light of the new policies, and I believe it’s not impossible to see them further evolve while maintaining the strength of their brands. In my opinion, we could be witnessing an accelerated focus on diversification similar to the premiumization that we see in alcoholic beverages companies, with more and more organic, zero-sugar, etc. options for everything they make in the future. For example, Pepsi has recently announced adding a new prebiotic cola to their portfolio, which is just one of many “new” trends in the industry, and this is where brand power and marketing come into play, allowing them to develop new products much faster than a new/unknown brand by including them under their umbrella.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diversified mix of peers - Kellanova (K), The Hershey Company (HSY), Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), Post Holdings (POST), Flowers Foods (FLO) and Kraft Heinz (KHC) - we see SJM somewhere in the middle-to-low side when it comes to most valuation metrics, which is similar to the stock’s performance compared to these peers.

Similarly, their margins are somewhere in the middle, with overall solid margins compared to peers (excluding the net income, which is negative due to the write-downs mentioned earlier). Especially if they reach their FCF target this year and the $1 billion goal as a result of lower CAPEX and improvements, there is potential to see them deliver better margins going forward.

Valuation

For J.M. Smucker’s DCF valuation, I’ll assume $850 million in free cash flow (2.85% lower than the company expects, to account for uncertainty with a better margin of safety). I will assume a 4% CAGR for the first 5 years (above inflation to account for their FCF growth initiatives), a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 6.5% - slightly above my estimate of 6.4% (assumptions: 8.01% cost of equity, 4.97% cost of debt, 21% US corporate tax rate) as well as the market’s for a better margin of safety. This way, we get to an enterprise value of approximately $19.59 billion, and when subtracting the $7.74 billion in net debt, this returns an equity value of roughly $11.85 billion. With 106.68 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of ~$111.08 per share. This is almost identical to the current price, so there is arguably little to no margin of safety, unfortunately.

Conclusions

Overall, I believe SJM is a Hold now based on their financials and relatively low potential, as well as no discount to my fair value estimate in a slightly conservative valuation. Their financials aren’t really attractive enough to justify a buy in such a mature industry that could be facing political pressure on top of the current economic one, and the dividend yield also doesn’t seem like enough of a reason alone. Still, it’s on my watchlist, and a potential drop after earnings could perhaps warrant a rating upgrade.

data.title: Silver Market Update - Big Potential Upside
data.description:
By Clive Maund

We got the upside breakout from the Bull Flag that was predicted in the last update posted in early July.

But then, rather like Apollo 12, which got struck by lightning twice in quick succession on the way up, the ride got bumpy for a while, with it reacting back quite sharply to support within the Flag and at the rising 50-day moving average.

But then it stabilized and started higher again last week, as we can see on the latest 6-month chart below.

And it is worth noting that the rather unexpectedly large reaction brought the MACD back to a very modest reading, which means that upside potential has been almost fully restored, and given the overall strongly bullish tenor of this chart, silver has big upside potential from here.

We are somewhat hampered looking at the silver chart in Stockwatch because volume and volume indicators are not available, but we can easily get around this obstacle by looking at the chart for the iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV), which is a reliable proxy for silver itself.

Thus, on the 6-month chart for SLV, we see that the volume pattern is strongly bullish, with big volume on rallies that dies back on the reactions that follow, which is why the Accumulation line shown at the top of the chart is trending strongly higher.

And the fact that it is already making new highs is certainly a propitious indication that points to a strong advance whose first target will be the 2011 all-time highs at $50 that, once cleared, can be expected to lead to rapid upside acceleration.

Zooming out via the 7-year chart, we see that the major bull market in silver is now becoming established and is poised to accelerate, with the price now advancing away from the giant Head-and-Shoulders continuation pattern that formed following the 2020 and 2021 highs.

With the price also being projected higher at an accelerating rate by the boundary of the giant Bowl pattern shown that is shepherding the price ever more rapidly towards a breakout above the resistance approaching and at the 2011 highs at $50.

The next two paragraphs are the same as in the last update because they have the same relevance...

On the long-term chart going back to the start of the millennium, i.e., to the start of 2000, we can see the origins of the resistance that silver is currently working its way through, which is the top that formed in 2011-2012.

Once it breaks clear above the high point of this, which is at $50, it will be in new high ground and free to accelerate away to the upside.

Lastly, it’s worth taking a look at the silver over gold ratio chart for the same time period, from 2000, which shows that despite gold’s big gains from early last year, this ratio is still at a very low level, which means that this sector bull market has much further to go. This sort of low reading means that there remains very little retail interest in the sector - this is very bullish for the sector and for silver in particular, which is regarded as a “steal” at these prices.

The rationale behind interpreting this chart is this: when there is a lot of speculative interest in the PM sector, investors favor silver over gold, because it has the capacity to make bigger percentage gains faster. This is what we saw when the sector peaked in 2011, with silver hitting $50 in the late Spring of that year and gold topping out later in the year in September, which is why the silver over gold ratio hit a peak.

When, on the other hand, the silver over gold ratio is at a low level, it means that speculative interest in the sector is at a low ebb, investors have no interest in it - which is very bullish, as it means that there is the potential for it to go much higher.

This is what we saw at the ratio lows in 2003 before the sector headed much higher, at the lows of the 2008 broad market crash which dragged the PMs down with it, and at the time of the Covid Crash in the Spring of 2020 when we saw a freak low due to the orchestrated mass psychosis that existed at that time.

Finally, an important point to make in closing is that the “window of opportunity” to buy silver and silver-related investments before the big breakout above $50 that is expected to lead to potentially dramatic acceleration is believed to be rapidly closing up, as a breakout above $50 is looking increasingly likely soon.
